message:
fwc/audit_chain: recompute event object_id during verify_chain to close producer-supplied id bypass

`verify_chain` consumed `AuditEventRecord { object_id: ObjectId,
event: AuditEvent }` records that come straight from JSONL via
`parse_event_records` and treated `record.object_id` as a trusted
identifier:

* The duplicate-seq / fork detection at line 502 keys
  `seen_seq` on the supplied `record.object_id`.
* The prev-pointer check at line 554 compares
  `record.event.prev` against `Some(&prev.object_id)` — the
  *previous record's supplied* object_id, not the canonical content
  hash of the previous event.
* The head match at line 583 compares
  `head.head_event` against `last.object_id`.

Every one of those checks runs against attacker-chosen tokens when
the JSONL itself is attacker-controlled. An attacker who can write a
crafted audit log can:

1. Construct a sequence of `AuditEvent { seq, prev, ... }` records
   with arbitrary content.
2. Set each record's `object_id` field to whatever the next record
   declares as its `prev`.
3. Run `fwc audit verify` against the JSONL — it reports `Ok` even
   though the supplied object_ids do not match the canonical
   BLAKE3-over-canonical-CBOR derivation that real producers use.

This is not a hash-collision attack — it is a hash-bypass attack.
The verifier never derives the hash, so any pair of strings that
"look like" valid IDs to each other passes. Reordering does not
help an attacker because seq + prev still has to chain, but the
chain that gets verified is one made of fictitious ids attached to
real-or-forged content.

Fix:

1. Add `computed_event_object_id(event)` — derives the ObjectId the
   way every other content-addressed object in fcp-core derives it:
   `fcp_cbor::to_canonical_cbor(event)` (deterministic per RFC 8949
   §4.2) hashed via `ObjectId::from_unscoped_bytes` (BLAKE3 with the
   `FCP2-CONTENT-V2` domain separator). Returns `Option` because
   canonical-CBOR can fail in adversarial cases (oversized payloads
   that exceed `MAX_CANONICAL_OBJECT_BYTES`).

2. In the existing per-record loop in `verify_chain`, before the
   `seen_seq` insertion, recompute the canonical id and compare to
   `record.object_id`:
   * `Some(expected)` and `expected != record.object_id` →
     `audit.object_id_mismatch` (`audit.fork_detected`-class
     severity — see below).
   * `None` (canonical-CBOR could not encode the event) →
     `audit.object_id_unverifiable`. Should not fire under normal
     operation; if it does, the verifier can no longer attest to the
     id, so treating it as a hard fail is correct.
   * Match (id matches the canonical derivation) → fall through, the
     existing checks can keep using `record.object_id` as a trusted
     alias for content.

3. Add both new codes to the `is_fail` match list at line 659 so
   they upgrade the report to `AuditVerifyStatus::Fail`. They are
   integrity issues, not warnings — a forged id is the same class of
   compromise as a fork or prev-mismatch.

Compatibility: existing tests in
`crates/fwc/src/audit_chain/mod.rs:1383-...` only call
`verify_chain(&[], None, None)`. The fixture JSONL loader
`load_audit_events` returns `AuditEventOutput` (a different type),
not `AuditEventRecord`, so it does not flow into `verify_chain`. No
production caller fabricates `AuditEventRecord` with a stub
`object_id`. Real producers (whenever they wire up — currently no
fcp-host code emits `AuditEvent`s; see audit notes below) MUST
supply the canonical-derivation id, which is the only id the new
check accepts.

Validation note: workspace-wide `cargo test -p fwc` was attempted
under `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/fcp-cc1` but currently fails to compile
through `fcp-conformance`, which has an in-progress uncommitted edit
in `crates/fcp-conformance/src/harness.rs` (another agent is wiring
gossip signature verification into the test harness and has a
`fcp_tailscale::NodeId` vs `fcp_core::NodeId` mismatch they have not
finished resolving). Per the AGENTS.md "never disturb other agents'
work" rule, I cannot land a workaround for that crate, so this
commit's fix was syntactically reviewed but not run-tested against
the rest of the workspace. The fix is mechanical: imports
`fcp_cbor::to_canonical_cbor`, calls `ObjectId::from_unscoped_bytes`
(both already-used patterns elsewhere in the codebase, e.g.
`crates/fcp-core/src/policy.rs:3032` and
`crates/fcp-core/src/checkpoint.rs:414`).

Audit notes for the rest of the chain integrity surface:

* `fcp-core::AuditEvent` (re-exported through `fcp-evidence`) IS
  hash-linked in the proper sense: `prev: Option<ObjectId>` is a
  32-byte BLAKE3 hash, each event carries `signature: NodeSignature`,
  and `AuditEvent::follows(other, other_id)` correctly compares
  `self.prev == Some(other_id)`. The hole was that no production
  function recomputed `other_id` for the verifier — `follows` takes
  it as a parameter, expecting the caller to derive it. fwc's
  `verify_chain` was the caller, and it was the one trusting the
  pre-supplied value. After this fix, fwc's verify_chain is the
  reference implementation of "compute every event's id, then walk
  the chain".

* `fcp-audit::verify_chain` (separate crate, used only by fwc per
  `Grep "use fcp_audit" crates`) operates on `AuditEntry { id:
  String, prev: Option<String> }` — no content addressing at the
  type level. Its docstring claims "hash-linked audit event" but
  the type permits arbitrary ids. Out of scope here; the canonical
  type is `fcp-core::AuditEvent`.

* Hash-collision attacks against BLAKE3 require ~2^128 work and are
  infeasible. The fix is not protecting against collision; it is
  protecting against the trivial supply-the-hash-yourself bypass.

* Reordering: `seq.checked_add(1) == record.event.seq` and
  `record.event.prev == Some(&prev.object_id)` still gate ordering
  correctly. With the new content-id check in front, attempting to
  reorder requires either fixing seqs (caught) or fixing prev links
  (caught) AND fixing each event's content to produce the matching
  canonical id (now infeasible without a hash collision).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
